markdown.markdown:
```{=html}
This section addresses the limitations surrounding the data used to complete this project.
``` The most fundamental limitation of this analysis is that formation choices in NFL games are not randomly assigned. Teams call Shotgun Runs in specific strategic contexts, often audibling when defenses are in pass-prevent alignments, when disguising run intentions from a spread formation provides a tactical advantage, or when the offense believes the defense has over-committed to pass coverage. This means that the 39,915 Shotgun Run plays in the training data are a systematically selected subset of situations, not a representative sample of what would happen if Shotgun Run were called randomly across all situations. This selection bias affects every formation-play type but with different magnitudes. Under Center Pass plays are called disproportionately in play-action situations against run-committed defenses, inflating their observed EPA relative to what would occur if Under Center Pass were called in neutral situations. The bootstrap analysis of second and long Under Center Pass plays found a mean EPA of +0.127 — a figure that likely reflects the favorable defensive alignments these plays typically face rather than the intrinsic value of the formation choice. A randomized experiment assigning formations without regard to situational context would produce different performance estimates, but such experimental designs are neither feasible nor ethical in NFL game settings. The appropriate response to this limitation is cautious interpretation rather than flat rejection of the model. The models accurately predict yards and EPA to expect given historical play-calling patterns in each situation. They should be understood as answering the question of what outcomes teams have historically achieved when calling each formation in each situation, not what outcomes would occur if formations were called in structurally different contexts. Coaches using the system should treat recommendations as informed suggestions using historical performance rather than causal estimates of formation effects. ## Missing Variables The seven variable feature set captures game state adequately but omits several factors that professional NFL analysts and coaching staffs treat as primary inputs to formation decisions. Adjusting to the defensive personnel package, whether the defense has deployed base, nickel, dime, or goal-line personnel, is arguably the single most important observable pre-snap factor in formation selection. Calling Shotgun Pass against dime coverage creates fundamentally different dynamics than calling it against base defense, yet the model treats both identically. Similarly, offensive personnel grouping, pre-snap motion usage, weather conditions for outdoor stadiums, and specific matchup information between offensive skill players and defensive backs are absent from the feature set by virtue of their unavailability in the public nflfastR database. The omission of player-level quality beyond quarterback also constrains model precision. The QB quality score introduced in this project captures one dimension of personnel advantage, but receiver corps quality, offensive line pass protection ratings, and running back effectiveness against specific defensive fronts all influence formation-play type EPA in ways the models cannot capture. Future work incorporating Next Gen Stats player tracking data or proprietary performance ratings could address these gaps, though at the cost of accessibility to public data that makes this analysis replicable. ## Temporal Validity The training period spanning 2016 through 2023 captured significant offensive evolution, but models trained on this window may inadequately represent the current state of NFL offense and defense. Defensive adaptation to shotgun formations has accelerated since 2020, with increasingly sophisticated disguised coverages and hybrid defensive personnel packages specifically designed to counter spread concepts. The systematic overestimation of Shotgun Run conversion probability in the 20-40 percent range during test set calibration analysis reflected exactly this temporal lag. Runs from shotgun that converted at certain rates during 2016-2018 became less effective as defenses adapted, and models trained on the full historical window incorporated those earlier rates alongside the declining recent ones. Implementation of this recommendation system would require periodic model retraining on rolling windows of recent seasons, dropping the earliest years of the training window as new seasons accumulate. An annual retraining cycle updating all eight Random Forest models on the most recent four or five seasons could maintain calibration against the current offensive and defensive environment. This limitation is common to all sports prediction models and does not fundamentally undermine the analytical approach, but it does constrain the shelf life of static models in a constantly evolving league. ## Pistol Formation Classification A technical limitation inherited from the nflfastR data source affects the precision of the shotgun classification. The binary shotgun indicator in nflfastR aggregates both traditional shotgun formations, where the quarterback aligns five to seven yards behind the center, and pistol formations, where the quarterback aligns three to four yards behind center with a running back immediately behind them. Pistol formations have unique blocking angles, run path options, and play-action mechanics that differentiate them from both traditional shotgun and under center alignments. Lumping pistol plays into the shotgun category introduces classification noise into the Shotgun Run and Shotgun Pass models, potentially obscuring differences between these structurally distinct approaches to quarterback alignment. Teams with heavy pistol usage, like the Baltimore Ravens and San Francisco 49ers, may show systematically different performance patterns within the Shotgun Run category that the current architecture cannot resolve. This limitation could be addressed through supplementary personnel data sources that distinguish pistol from shotgun alignments at the play level, though such data was not available at the scale needed for this analysis. ## Scope of the Recommendation System The recommendation system as designed optimizes for EPA and conversion probability given the situational inputs provided. It does not account for several strategic considerations that coaching staffs weigh in selecting formations. Establishing running game effectiveness to create play-action opportunities later in the game is a well-documented strategic rationale for calling Under Center Runs with negative expected EPA. Making the decision to sacrifice the value of one play for success later in the game is something made by coaches and while the system cannot model strategic considerations outside of a single-play perspective, it presents coaches with the information to make those decisions themselves. Similarly, the system treats each play call as independent rather than as part of a drive or game-level sequence. Down and distance situations within a drive are directly correlated – a failed Shotgun Run on first down creates the second and long context that the system then analyzes independently. A more complete model would incorporate drive-level or game-level optimization, recommending formation strategies across sequences of plays rather than optimizing each play in isolation. This extension, while theoretically appealing, would substantially increase model complexity and was beyond the scope of this initial analysis. The current system is best understood as a situational baseline recommendation tool that quantifies historical formation-play type performance and communicates uncertainty transparently, providing coaches and analysts with an evidence-based starting point for formation decisions that they will appropriately adjust based on game-specific context the models cannot observe.
